draft.introduction: Utterances 273–274, the “Cannibal Hymn,” describe the dead king hunting, slaughtering, and consuming the gods to absorb their power—a theology of divine assimilation without parallel in later Egyptian religion.

translation.text: The sky is overcast, the stars are darkened. The celestial expanses quake, the bones of the earth-gods tremble. All motion ceases when they see the King appearing in power, a god who lives on his fathers and feeds on his mothers. The King is the bull of the sky.
translation.original_text: nbi p.t, sḥd sbā.w, ḥmm(ā.w) āḫw, gm(.w) qs.w tā-wr.w, gr mnmn.wt māā.sn nsw.t ḫā m bāw, wnm j.t(j).w=f, ām(.w) mw.wt=f

translator_notes.note: The “Cannibal Hymn” describes the king eating the gods to absorb their ba (soul-power), akh (luminous spirit), and heka (magical power). This theophagic theology—becoming divine by consuming divinity—disappears from later Egyptian religion but represents the most radical ancient assertion of human-divine fusion.
